title: Chapter 7: The Watcher
content: The Patriarch invited him to tea.

In the Liang family, this was roughly equivalent to being summoned to the principal's office—if the principal was a six-hundred-year-old dynasty's worth of secrets and the office was a private study lined with artifacts that could level city blocks.

Ren walked the long corridor to the Patriarch's wing with his enhanced senses mapped to full alert. The household security was thicker here—guards at every corner, array signatures pulsing from the walls like hidden tripwires. The deeper into the estate's core he went, the stronger the dimensional resonance became, a subsonic hum that pressed against his eardrums like the breath of something enormous.
The study door was hand-carved camphor wood. A servant opened it without knocking—an indication that Ren's arrival had been tracked from the moment he left his room.

"Mr. Zhou." Liang Tianming stood by the window, silhouetted against the afternoon sun. At sixty-three, he carried his age like armor—silver hair swept back, spine straight, hands folded behind his back in a pose that managed to suggest both contemplation and the patience of a predator. "Please. Sit."
The tea was already poured. Oolong, Ren noted—high-mountain, probably Alishan. The Patriarch didn't serve cheap tea to people he was about to interrogate.
Ren sat. The chair was lower than the Patriarch's—a power play so classic it was almost quaint.

"I wanted to thank you." Liang Tianming settled into his own seat with the unhurried grace of a man who controlled the clock in every room he entered. "For what you did for my daughter last week. The assassination attempt... troubling. Your quick thinking may have saved her life."
"It was instinct."

"Was it?" The Patriarch's eyes—a deeper, older version of Feng's—settled on Ren with the weight of a searchlight. "I've reviewed the security footage, Mr. Zhou. Your reactions were... remarkable. Not the flinching of a bystander, but the calculated movement of someone who's been trained to respond to gunfire."
*He's testing me.*

Ren had known this moment would come. A man didn't run the most powerful family in Crystal City for forty years without developing a keen sense for anomalies. And Ren was very much an anomaly—the bankrupt son-in-law who'd solved a quarter-billion-yuan logistics problem from a library, then dodged a sniper's bullet with preternatural precision.
"I grew up in a rough neighborhood," Ren said mildly. "You learn to read the room."

"Perhaps." The Patriarch sipped his tea. "And perhaps there's more to the Zhou family's disappointment of a son than meets the eye. I've had my people look into your background, Mr. Zhou. Thoroughly. Your academic record is unimpressive. Your employment history, nonexistent. Your family's finances, catastrophic. And yet—"
He set down his cup with a soft click.
"—and yet, you refuse a divorce that would set you free. You solve problems my daughter's entire team couldn't unravel. You dodge bullets with the composure of a soldier. And my son—"
A pause. The silence in the study was absolute.
"—my son has been... different, since your conversation in the garden. More subdued. Less explosive. He told me you knew things about his sleep. About his dreams."
Ren kept his expression neutral. *Feng told his father about the garden. Of course he did. This family reports everything upward.*
"Your son isn't sleeping well," Ren said carefully. "That's not a secret. It's visible to anyone who pays attention."
"And you pay attention." The Patriarch's gaze sharpened. "To everything, it seems."
*Here it comes.*
"I'm a guest in this household, sir. Guests observe."

"You're not a guest. You're family." The Patriarch said the word *family* the way a priest said *sacred*—with the weight of generations behind it. "Which is precisely why I'm curious about your... capabilities."

He stood and walked to a cabinet against the far wall. From it, he removed a folder—"Paper," the Patriarch said, following Ren's glance. "Not digital. Not reproducible. Some conversations are too sensitive for electrons."
He placed the folder on the table between them. Didn't open it.

"In the past six months, Mr. Zhou, I've had three different intelligence assessments flag you as a potential security risk. A month ago, I would have agreed with them. Today—" He studied Ren's face with the clinical detachment of a man reading a balance sheet. "Today, I'm not certain what you are. But I know what you're not. You're not a fool. You're not a coward. And you're not here for the money."
Ren said nothing. The Salvation System was running analysis on every micro-expression, every word choice, every array pulse in the room:

`SUBJECT: Liang Tianming`
`INTENT: Evaluation — not elimination`
`THREAT LEVEL: Critical (do not underestimate)`
`SURVEILLANCE: This study contains 4 array-based monitoring nodes`
`NOTE: The Patriarch already knows more than he's revealing. He's testing not what you know, but whether you know THAT he knows.`
*He's three moves ahead,* Ren realized. *And he's inviting me to show him how many moves I can see.*
"Sir," Ren said slowly, "I'm going to be honest with you."
The Patriarch's eyebrow rose a fraction of an inch—genuine surprise. People in this family didn't volunteer honesty.

"I married your daughter because I was asked to. I stayed because—honestly, at first, because I had nowhere else to go. But I'm staying now because I believe this family is facing threats it doesn't fully understand, and I can help."
"What threats?"
"The Zhao family is the obvious one. But they're just the tip. There are... forces in this city that operate below the surface of corporate politics. Forces that most people can't see or sense."
*Careful. He's recording this. Every word.*

Liang Tianming's expression didn't change. But his fingers—resting on the folder—tapped once. Twice. A rhythm that the Salvation System identified as a tell: the Patriarch's version of increased heart rate.
"Go on."
"I can't prove any of this. Not yet. But I can demonstrate value. As I have been." Ren leaned forward slightly. "You don't need to trust me, sir. You just need to keep me close enough to be useful."
A long silence. The Patriarch's eyes bored into Ren's—measuring, calculating, running probability assessments that would make a supercomputer look approximate.
Then, impossibly, Liang Tianming smiled.
It was the smile of a man who'd just confirmed a hypothesis he hadn't shared.

"Useful," the Patriarch repeated. "Yes. I think you may be." He picked up the folder and returned it to the cabinet. "My daughter has requested that you be given access to the family's eastern intelligence network. Limited access. I've approved it."
The Salvation System blazed:

`🟢 ACCESS UPGRADE DETECTED`
`CAUSAL KEY: Spousal recognition + Patriarch approval`
`NEW ACCESS: Eastern Intelligence Network ( surveillance, communications intercepts, corporate intelligence)`
`ARRAY BACKDOOR: Expanded. Salvation System can now passively scan Liang family data traffic.`
`WARNING: This access comes with monitoring. The Patriarch will be watching what you access.`
`NOTE: He gave you access BECAUSE he wants to see what you look for. You are being used as a probe. But so is he.`
*We're probing each other,* Ren understood. *A game of mutual intelligence, where we both pretend we don't know the other is playing.*
"Thank you, sir."

"Don't thank me. Be useful." The Patriarch returned to his window. "And Mr. Zhou—my son's dreams. If you learn anything more about what's troubling him, I expect to hear it from you directly. Not through servants. Not through my daughter. From *you*."
The dismissal was implicit. Ren rose, bowed slightly, and left.
In the corridor outside, he exhaled. His hands weren't shaking—but they wanted to.
The Patriarch had given him access because he *wanted* Ren to find something. That meant the Patriarch had secrets he wanted discovered—or secrets he wanted to confirm that Ren already knew.
*He's using me as a canary in the coal mine. If I find his portal, he'll know someone's looking for it. If I don't find it, he'll know I'm not the threat he worried about.*
*Unless I find it and pretend I didn't.*
The game was getting complicated. But complicated was where Ren Zhou operated best.

He walked back through the estate with his new access credentials humming in the Salvation System's interface, already scanning data streams, filtering for anomalies, building a picture of the Liang family's operations that no outsider had ever seen.
And in the depths of the restricted wing, the dimensional array hummed its patient, ancient song.
One thousand eighty-eight days.
